468	\begin{lemma}[Abelian universal property of the relative tensor product]
469	  \label{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
470	  \lean{TensorProduct.liftAddHom}
471	  \mathlibok
472	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.TensorProduct.Basic}).}
473	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N, P\) be \(S\)-modules (or, more
474	  generally, the abelian-group statement: \(M, N\) modules and \(P\) an additive
475	  commutative group). An additive-bilinear map
476	  \(f : M \to (N \to P)\) that is \emph{\(S\)-balanced}, i.e.\ satisfies
477	  \(f(s \cdot m)(n) = f(m)(s \cdot n)\) for all \(s \in S\), factors uniquely
478	  through the relative tensor product as an additive group homomorphism
479	  \(\widetilde{f} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with
480	  \(\widetilde{f}(m \otimes_S n) = f(m)(n)\). This is the abelian-group universal
481	  property of \(M \otimes_S N\): it is the quotient of \(M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N\)
482	  by the \(S\)-balancing relations.
483	\end{lemma}

485	\begin{lemma}[The relative tensor of modules is a coequalizer of abelian groups]
486	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
487	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.isColimitCofork,
488	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actN,
489	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actM,
490	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap,
491	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap,
492	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL,
493	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_surjective,
494	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_comp_act,
495	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aL,
496	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.aR,
497	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor,
498	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_epi,
499	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.coeq_condition,
500	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.cofork,
501	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom,
502	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descMor,
503	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descFac,
504	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actLmap_tmul,
505	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.actRmap_tmul,
506	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.projL_tmul,
507	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.piMor_apply,
508	        AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.RelativeTensorCoequalizer.descHom_tmul}
509	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
510	  Let \(S\) be a commutative ring and \(M, N\) be \(S\)-modules. The abelian group
511	  \(M \otimes_S N\) is the coequalizer, \emph{in the category
512	  \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of abelian groups}, of the two parallel
513	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear \(S\)-action maps
514	  \[
515	    a_L,\, a_R :
516	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N)
517	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
518	    M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N,
519	  \]
520	  where the \emph{left} action map sends \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto
521	  (s \cdot m) \otimes n\) and the \emph{right} action map sends
522	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n) \mapsto m \otimes (s \cdot n)\). The coforking map is
523	  the canonical projection \(\pi : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to M \otimes_S N\),
524	  \(m \otimes n \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
525	\end{lemma}
526	
527	\begin{proof}
528	  \uses{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib}
529	  Write \(a_N : S \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to N\) and
530	  \(a_M : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} S \to M\) for the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-linear maps
531	  induced by the scalar action (\(s \otimes n \mapsto s \cdot n\),
532	  \(m \otimes s \mapsto s \cdot m\)); the right action map is
533	  \(a_R = \mathrm{id}_M \otimes a_N\) and the left action map is
534	  \(a_L = (a_M \otimes \mathrm{id}_N) \circ \mathrm{assoc}^{-1}\), where
535	  \(\mathrm{assoc}\) is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor associator. The projection
536	  \(\pi\) is the additive homomorphism produced by the abelian universal property
537	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} from the \(S\)-balanced bilinear map
538	  \((m, n) \mapsto m \otimes_S n\).
539	
540	  \emph{Cofork condition.} On an elementary tensor
541	  \(m \otimes (s \otimes n)\) we have
542	  \(\pi(a_L(m \otimes s \otimes n)) = (s \cdot m) \otimes_S n
543	  = m \otimes_S (s \cdot n) = \pi(a_R(m \otimes s \otimes n))\), the middle
544	  equality being the defining \(S\)-balancing relation of \(M \otimes_S N\); since
545	  elementary tensors generate, \(\pi \circ a_L = \pi \circ a_R\).
546	
547	  \emph{Universal property.} Given any abelian group \(P\) and a homomorphism
548	  \(g : M \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} N \to P\) with \(g \circ a_L = g \circ a_R\), the
549	  bilinear map \((m, n) \mapsto g(m \otimes n)\) is \(S\)-balanced (that is exactly
550	  the cofork condition read on elementary tensors), so by
551	  \cref{lem:tensorProduct_liftAddHom_mathlib} it factors as a unique
552	  \(\widetilde{g} : M \otimes_S N \to P\) with \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi = g\).
553	  Existence is this descended map; uniqueness follows because \(\pi\) is
554	  surjective, hence an epimorphism of abelian groups, so \(\widetilde{g} \circ \pi
555	  = g\) determines \(\widetilde{g}\). Packaging existence and uniqueness gives the
556	  colimit cofork.
557	
558	\end{proof}
559	
560	\begin{lemma}[Relative tensor of module presheaves as a coequalizer]
561	  \label{lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer}
562	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.relativeTensorCoequalizerIso}
563	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
564	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
565	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
566	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
567	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
568	  underlying abelian-group presheaf of their relative tensor product
569	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
570	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) is naturally the coequalizer of the
571	  parallel pair
572	  \[
573	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
574	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
575	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
576	  \]
577	  of abelian-group presheaves, where the first map acts the ring factor on the left,
578	  \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto (r \cdot p) \otimes q\), and the second on the
579	  right, \(p \otimes r \otimes q \mapsto p \otimes (r \cdot q)\). The identification
580	  is objectwise and natural in \(U\).
581	\end{lemma}
582	
583	\begin{proof}
584	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
585	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
586	    lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib,
587	    lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}
588	  The argument promotes the objectwise coequalizer of
589	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} to the functor category, in
590	  three steps.
591	
592	  \textbf{1. Objectwise.} For each open \(U\), apply
593	  \cref{lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer} with \(S = \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
594	  \(M = \mathcal{P}(U)\), \(N = \mathcal{Q}(U)\): the abelian group
595	  \(\mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is the coequalizer
596	  in \(\mathrm{AddCommGrp}\) of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action maps
597	  \[
598	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
599	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
600	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
601	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
602	  \]
603	  with cofork map the projection \(\pi_U\).
604	
605	  \textbf{2. Naturality / promotion.} The three objectwise families assemble into
606	  morphisms of abelian-group presheaves
607	  \[
608	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
609	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
610	    \;\overset{a_L,\,a_R}{\rightrightarrows}\;
611	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
612	    \;\overset{\pi}{\longrightarrow}\;
613	    \bigl(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\bigr),
614	  \]
615	  i.e.\ natural transformations of functors
616	  \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\). Naturality of
617	  \(a_L, a_R, \pi\) in \(U\) is the compatibility of the module action with the
618	  restriction maps of \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{O}_X, \mathcal{Q}\): the restriction
619	  of \(s \cdot m\) is \((s|_V) \cdot (m|_V)\), and likewise for the projection,
620	  because the relative tensor is functorial in \(U\). Colimits in a functor
621	  category are computed objectwise --- by
622	  \cref{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib} a cocone is a colimit as
623	  soon as every evaluation \((\mathrm{ev}_U)\) of it is --- so the objectwise
624	  coequalizers of step 1 assemble into a coequalizer of the displayed parallel
625	  pair in \(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathrm{AddCommGrp}\).
626	
627	  \textbf{3. Identification of the apex.} The apex presheaf
628	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\) is, by the
629	  objectwise tensor formula of the presheaf monoidal structure
630	  (\cref{lem:presheaf_tensorObj_obj_mathlib}: \((\mathcal{M}_1 \otimes_p
631	  \mathcal{M}_2)(U) = \mathcal{M}_1(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}
632	  \mathcal{M}_2(U)\)), exactly the underlying abelian-group presheaf of the
633	  relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\)
634	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Transporting the colimit of step 2
635	  along this identification exhibits
636	  \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{Q}\), as an abelian-group
637	  presheaf, as the coequalizer of the parallel pair, naturally in \(U\).
638	\end{proof}
639	
640	\begin{lemma}[Objectwise colimits in a functor category]
641	  \label{lem:evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits_mathlib}
642	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Limits.evaluationJointlyReflectsColimits}
643	  \mathlibok
644	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
645	  (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Limits.FunctorCategory.Basic}).}
646	  Let \(\mathcal{D}\) be a category and \(\mathcal{A}\) a category in which the
647	  relevant colimits exist. A cocone over a diagram
648	  \(F : \mathcal{J} \to (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A})\) in the functor category is
649	  a colimit cocone as soon as, for every object \(U\) of \(\mathcal{D}\), its image
650	  under evaluation \(\mathrm{ev}_U : (\mathcal{D} \to \mathcal{A}) \to \mathcal{A}\)
651	  is a colimit cocone in \(\mathcal{A}\). Equivalently, colimits in a functor
652	  category are computed objectwise.
653	\end{lemma}

657	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj_obj}
658	  \mathlibok

673	\begin{lemma}[Sheafification inverts the whiskered localization unit]
674	  \label{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
675	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
676	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
677	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
678	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
679	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
680	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer}
681	  Let \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{Q}\) be presheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules,
682	  let \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to (\mathcal{P})^{\#}\) be the unit of
683	  the sheafification adjunction
684	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}), viewed as a morphism of
685	  presheaves of modules, and write
686	  \[
687	    \eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} :
688	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
689	    \longrightarrow (\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}
690	  \]
691	  for its right whiskering by \(\mathcal{Q}\) in the presheaf monoidal structure
692	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}). Then the image of
693	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) under the sheafification functor,
694	  \[
695	    (\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#} :
696	    (\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q})^{\#}
697	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
698	    \bigl((\mathcal{P})^{\#} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\bigr)^{\#},
699	  \]
700	  is an isomorphism of sheaves of modules. This is the strong-monoidality
701	  comparison of the module sheafification functor on a whiskered unit.
702	\end{lemma}
703	
704	\begin{proof}
705	  \uses{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
706	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib,
707	    lem:isIso_sheafification_map_iff, lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit,
708	    lem:relativeTensor_as_coequalizer,
709	    lem:relativeTensor_objectwise_coequalizer,
710	    lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf}
711	  Module sheafification is a localization functor: it inverts exactly the class
712	  \(W\) of morphisms of presheaves of modules whose underlying morphism of
713	  abelian-group presheaves is a local isomorphism --- that is, lies in the class
714	  \(J.W\) of weak equivalences attached to the Grothendieck topology \(J\) on
715	  \(X\) (the inverse image of \(J.W\) along the forgetful functor to
716	  abelian-group presheaves). By the localization criterion for a reflective
717	  adjunction, \((f)^{\#}\) is an isomorphism if and only if \(f \in W\), i.e.\
718	  if and only if the underlying abelian-presheaf morphism of \(f\) lies in
719	  \(J.W\). It therefore suffices to prove that the underlying abelian morphism
720	  of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) lies in \(J.W\).
721	
722	  \textbf{The relative tensor as a coequalizer.} The underlying abelian-group
723	  presheaf of the relative tensor product \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\)
724	  is the objectwise relative tensor
725	  \(U \mapsto \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)} \mathcal{Q}(U)\). For each
726	  \(U\) this module is the coequalizer of the two \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-action
727	  maps
728	  \[
729	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X(U)
730	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U)
731	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
732	    \mathcal{P}(U) \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}(U),
733	  \]
734	  one acting on the left and one on the right factor. Colimits of abelian-group
735	  presheaves are computed objectwise, so \(\mathcal{P} \otimes_p \mathcal{Q}\),
736	  as an abelian-group presheaf, is the coequalizer of the corresponding parallel
737	  pair
738	  \[
739	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{O}_X
740	      \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
741	    \;\rightrightarrows\;
742	    \mathcal{P} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{Q}
743	  \]
744	  of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaves. Under this presentation the underlying
745	  abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) is the map of
746	  coequalizers induced by whiskering the two rows with
747	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} (-)\).
748	
749	  \textbf{Abelian sheafification preserves the coequalizer.} The abelian
750	  sheafification functor \(a\) (the associated-sheaf functor for \(J\) on
751	  abelian-group presheaves) is a left adjoint, hence preserves coequalizers; it
752	  therefore carries the parallel pair and its induced map to the analogous data
753	  of \(a\)-images. It remains to see that the two whiskered comparison maps
754	  relating the rows,
755	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) and
756	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}
757	  \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), lie in \(J.W\) (equivalently, become
758	  isomorphisms after applying \(a\)).
759	
760	  \textbf{Whiskered units are stalkwise isomorphisms.} We argue this via stalks
761	  of abelian-group presheaves (\(\mathrm{TopCat.Presheaf.stalk}\), which exist
762	  for abelian presheaves on the underlying space, unlike module-sheaf stalks).
763	  The sheafification unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} : \mathcal{P} \to a\mathcal{P}\)
764	  is a \emph{stalkwise isomorphism}: for each point \(x\) the stalk map
765	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x : \mathcal{P}_x \to (a\mathcal{P})_x\) is an
766	  isomorphism (the defining property of the associated-sheaf unit). The stalk of
767	  a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor presheaf is the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-tensor of stalks,
768	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B})_x \cong
769	  \mathcal{A}_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathcal{B}_x\), because the stalk is a
770	  filtered colimit and \(\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\) commutes with filtered colimits.
771	  Under this identification the stalk of
772	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\) at \(x\) is
773	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}})_x \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{id}\), an isomorphism
774	  (tensoring an isomorphism with the identity). Hence each whiskered unit is a
775	  stalkwise isomorphism, and a stalkwise isomorphism of abelian-group presheaves
776	  on a topological space is a local isomorphism, i.e.\ lies in \(J.W\)
777	  (\cref{lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso}). Applying \(a\) therefore turns
778	  both whiskered comparison maps into isomorphisms.
779	
780	  An isomorphism between two parallel-pair diagrams induces an isomorphism of
781	  their coequalizers; applied to the rows above, this shows that \(a\) sends the
782	  underlying abelian morphism of \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q}\) to an
783	  isomorphism. Hence that morphism lies in \(J.W\), so
784	  \(\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q} \in W\), and
785	  \((\eta_{\mathcal{P}} \rhd \mathcal{Q})^{\#}\) is an isomorphism.
786	\end{proof}

788	\begin{corollary}[Sheaf-level associator on tensor products]
789	  \label{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}
790	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjAssoc}
791	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
792	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
793	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
794	  For sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B},
795	  \mathcal{C}\) there is a canonical associativity isomorphism
796	  \[
797	    \alpha_{\mathcal{A},\mathcal{B},\mathcal{C}} :
798	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{B})
799	      \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C}
800	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
801	    \mathcal{A} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
802	      (\mathcal{B} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{C})
803	  \]
804	  of sheaves of modules (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}), natural in all three
805	  arguments.
806	\end{corollary}
807	
808	\begin{proof}
809	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
810	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
811	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
812	  Write \(a, b, c\) for the underlying presheaves of modules of
813	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) and \((-)^{\#}\) for sheafification
814	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}). By
815	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} the two iterated products unfold to
816	  \[
817	    (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
818	      = \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#},
819	    \qquad
820	    \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
821	      = \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#},
822	  \]
823	  each carrying an \emph{inner} sheafification that obstructs a direct appeal to
824	  the presheaf associator. The whiskered units remove these inner
825	  sheafifications. Applying \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}
826	  with \(\mathcal{P} = a \otimes_p b\) and \(\mathcal{Q} = c\), the morphism
827	  \[
828	    (\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#} :
829	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
830	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
831	    \bigl((a \otimes_p b)^{\#} \otimes_p c\bigr)^{\#}
832	    = (\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}
833	  \]
834	  is an isomorphism; and, reading the same lemma through the braiding of the
835	  presheaf monoidal structure (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}) so as
836	  to whisker the unit \(\eta_{b \otimes_p c}\) on the left of \(a\), the
837	  morphism
838	  \[
839	    (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#} :
840	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)\bigr)^{\#}
841	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
842	    \bigl(a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)^{\#}\bigr)^{\#}
843	    = \mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})
844	  \]
845	  is an isomorphism. Between the two un-sheafified middle terms sits the
846	  sheafification of the presheaf associator
847	  \[
848	    \alpha^p_{a,b,c} :
849	    (a \otimes_p b) \otimes_p c
850	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
851	    a \otimes_p (b \otimes_p c)
852	  \]
853	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), an isomorphism because
854	  sheafification is a functor and \(\alpha^p\) is one. The associator is the
855	  composite

858	    = (a \lhd \eta_{b \otimes_p c})^{\#}
859	      \circ (\alpha^p_{a,b,c})^{\#}
860	      \circ \bigl((\eta_{a \otimes_p b} \rhd c)^{\#}\bigr)^{-1},

863	  \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, \mathcal{C}\) follows from naturality of the unit,

867	\begin{lemma}[Tensor-power comparison isomorphism]
868	  \label{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
869	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorPowAdd}
870	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj}
871	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules"
872	  % (Tag 01CU, following `definition-powers') (read from
873	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4249-L4254).
874	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
875	  % "With this definition we have canonical isomorphisms
876	  %  $\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
877	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \to
878	  %  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m}$, and these isomorphisms
879	  %  satisfy a commutativity and an associativity constraint
880	  %  (formulation omitted)."
881	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CU (canonical
882	  power isomorphisms).}
883	  For a line bundle \(\mathcal{L}\) and \(m, m' \in \mathbb{N}\) there is a
884	  canonical isomorphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
885	  \[
886	    \mu_{m,m'} :
887	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
888	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m')},
889	  \]
890	  natural in \(\mathcal{L}\), and these isomorphisms satisfy a commutativity
891	  constraint (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) agrees with \(\mu_{m',m}\) after the braiding) and
892	  an associativity constraint (the two ways of combining
893	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
894	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''}\) into \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\) agree).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, def:sheafTensorObj, cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc,
899	    def:tensorObjUnitIso, def:tensorBraiding,
900	    lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit,
901	    lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
902	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
903	  We do \emph{not} build a full monoidal structure on the whole category
904	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) --- none is constructed here --- but only assemble
905	  the comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\) from those structural isomorphisms of
906	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} that the induction actually uses: the associator,
907	  the braiding, and the left unitor.
908	
909	  \textbf{The structural maps.} The braiding and the unitors descend to
910	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) directly: each is the sheafification of the
911	  corresponding isomorphism of the presheaf symmetric monoidal structure
912	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and sheafification, being a
913	  functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. The \emph{associator} is not
914	  the sheafification of a single presheaf isomorphism, because the iterated
915	  sheaf tensor products
916	  \((\mathcal{A} \otimes \mathcal{B}) \otimes \mathcal{C}\) and
917	  \(\mathcal{A} \otimes (\mathcal{B} \otimes \mathcal{C})\) each carry an inner
918	  sheafification (\cref{def:sheafTensorObj}); it is supplied instead by
919	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, whose construction clears those inner
920	  sheafifications using the strong-monoidality comparison
921	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit}. These are the only
922	  isomorphisms of sheaves of modules the construction invokes; right-exactness
923	  of the tensor product is never used.
924	
925	  \textbf{Construction of \(\mu_{m,m'}\).} Fix \(m'\) and induct on \(m\). For
926	  \(m = 0\),
927	  \[
928	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
929	      = \mathcal{O}_X \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
930	      \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
931	  \]
932	  is the left unitor of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}. For the inductive step the
933	  recursive definition
934	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}\)
935	  (\cref{def:sheafTensorPow}) gives
936	  \[
937	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+1)} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
938	    = (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}) \otimes
939	      \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}
940	    \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
941	      (\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'})
942	    \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{id} \otimes \beta\ } \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes
943	      (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes \mathcal{L}),
944	  \]
945	  using the associator \(\alpha\) (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and then the
946	  braiding \(\beta\) of \cref{def:sheafTensorObj} whiskered into the left
947	  factor. The inverse associator \(\alpha^{-1}\)
948	  (\cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}) and the inductive comparison \(\mu_{m,m'}\)
949	  whiskered by \(\mathcal{L}\) on the right then carry this to
950	  \[
951	    (\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'}) \otimes
952	      \mathcal{L}
953	    \xrightarrow{\ \mu_{m,m'} \rhd \mathcal{L}\ }
954	    \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m + m')} \otimes \mathcal{L}
955	    = \mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m + m') + 1)},
956	  \]
957	  the final equality being the recursive definition. The reindexing
958	  \((m + m') + 1 = (m + 1) + m'\) identifies the target with
959	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes ((m+1) + m')}\), and the composite is
960	  \(\mu_{m+1,m'}\), an isomorphism as a composite of isomorphisms. Naturality in
961	  \(\mathcal{L}\) holds because each constituent comparison is natural.
962	
963	  \textbf{Commutativity and associativity constraints.} The braiding and
964	  unitors are the sheafifications of the symmetric-monoidal coherence data of
965	  \(\PshMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\) (\cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib}), and
966	  the associator is intertwined with the presheaf associator by the comparison
967	  isomorphisms of \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} through the
968	  construction of \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}. Both the commutativity square
969	  (\(\mu_{m,m'}\) versus \(\mu_{m',m}\) postcomposed with the braiding) and the
970	  associativity pentagon (the two bracketings of
971	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes m} \otimes \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m'} \otimes
972	  \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m''} \to \mathcal{L}^{\otimes (m+m'+m'')}\)) are therefore
973	  images, under the sheafification functor together with these comparison
974	  isomorphisms, of the corresponding coherence diagrams of the presheaf
975	  symmetric monoidal structure, which commute by Mac\,Lane coherence. Since a
976	  functor preserves commuting diagrams and the comparison isomorphisms are
977	  natural, the constraints hold for the family \(\{\mu_{m,m'}\}\). We emphasise
978	  that this argument uses only the single strong-monoidality comparison
979	  \cref{lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit} and the resulting associator
980	  \cref{cor:sheafTensorObjAssoc}, not a full monoidal structure on
981	  \(\ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X)\).
982	\end{proof}

984	\section{Lax-monoidal global sections}
985	\label{sec:sgr_lax_sections}
986	
987	The graded multiplication is supplied by the lax-monoidal structure of the
988	global-sections functor \(\Gamma(X, -) : \ShMod(\mathcal{O}_X) \to
989	\mathrm{ModuleCat}(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X))\). It is only \emph{lax} because
990	global sections do not commute with sheafification: a global section of a
991	sheafified tensor product is not in general a finite sum of elementary tensors of
992	global sections.
993	
994	\begin{definition}\leanok
995	[Section multiplication]
996	  \label{def:sectionMul}
997	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul}
998	  \uses{def:sheafTensorObj, lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib,
999	    lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib}
1000	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The
1001	  \emph{section multiplication} is the \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-bilinear map
1002	  \[
1003	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)}
1004	      \Gamma(X, \mathcal{G})
1005	    \;\longrightarrow\;
1006	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})
1007	  \]
1008	  defined as follows. A pair of global sections \((\sigma, \tau)\) determines,
1009	  by the objectwise formula of \cref{lem:presheafModule_monoidal_mathlib} at the
1010	  top open, the elementary tensor \(\sigma \otimes \tau \in
1011	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})(X)\), a global section of
1012	  the tensor product presheaf. Composing with the global-sections component of
1013	  the sheafification unit
1014	  \(\eta : \mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G} \to
1015	  (\mathcal{F} \otimes_{p,\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})^{\#} =
1016	  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G}\)
1017	  (\cref{lem:presheafModule_sheafification_mathlib},
1018	  \cref{def:sheafTensorObj}) yields a global section
1019	  \(\sigma \cdot \tau \in
1020	  \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{G})\). Bilinearity over
1021	  \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is inherited from bilinearity of the elementary
1022	  tensor and \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\)-linearity of \(\eta\).
1023	\end{definition}
1024	
1025	\begin{lemma}[Coherence of the section multiplication]
1026	  \label{lem:sectionMul_coherent}
1027	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.sectionsMul_assoc_unit}
1028	  \uses{def:sectionMul, lem:moduleUnit_mathlib, lem:sheafTensorPow_add}
1029	  The section multiplications (\cref{def:sectionMul}) are associative and unital

970	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
971	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.

976	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.

979	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
980	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
981	
982	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
983	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
984	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
985	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
986	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
987	
988	\begin{lemma}\leanok
989	[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
990	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
991	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
992	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
993	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1001	\end{lemma}
1002	\begin{proof}
1003	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}
1011	
1012	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1013	[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1014	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1016	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1017	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1018	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1019	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1020	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1021	\end{lemma}
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1024	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1025	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1026	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1027	\end{proof}

1029	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1030	[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1031	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1032	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1033	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1034	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1035	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1036	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1037	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1038	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1039	\end{lemma}
1040	\begin{proof}
1041	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1042	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1043	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1044	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1045	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1046	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1047	  these conditions gives the claim.
1048	\end{proof}

1050	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1051	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1052	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1053	  \mathlibok
1054	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1055	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1056	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1057	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1058	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1059	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).

1062	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1063	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1064	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1065	  \mathlibok
1066	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1067	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1068	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1069	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1070	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).

1073	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1074	[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1075	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1076	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1077	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1078	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1079	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1080	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1081	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1082	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1083	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1084	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1085	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1086	\end{lemma}
1087	
1088	\begin{proof}
1089	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1090	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1091	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1092	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1093	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1094	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1095	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1096	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1097	
1098	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1099	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1100	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1101	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1102	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1103	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1104	
1105	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1106	  \[
1107	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1108	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1109	  \]
1110	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1111	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1112	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1113	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1114	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1115	
1116	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1117	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1118	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1119	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1120	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1121	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1122	\end{proof}
1123	
1124	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1125	[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1126	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1127	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1128	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1129	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1130	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1131	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1132	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1133	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1134	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1135	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1136	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1137	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1138	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1139	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1140	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1141	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1142	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1143	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1144	  core of the induction).}
1145	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1146	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1147	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1148	
1149	\end{lemma}
1150	
1151	\begin{proof}
1152	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1153	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1154	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1155	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1156	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1157	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1158	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1159	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1160	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1161	
1162	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1163	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1164	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1165	
1166	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1167	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1168	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1169	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1170	  \begin{enumerate}
1171	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1172	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1173	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1174	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1175	      \(P_d\)-modules
1176	      \[
1177	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1178	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1179	      \]
1180	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1181	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1182	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1183	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1184	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1185	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1186	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1187	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1188	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1189	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1190	      to \(A_g\).
1191	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1192	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1193	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1194	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1195	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1196	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1197	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1198	      over \(A_f\).
1199	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1200	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1201	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1202	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1203	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1204	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1205	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1206	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1207	      completes the induction.
1208	  \end{enumerate}
1209	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open.
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}[Finiteness is local on a finite spanning family]
1267	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1268	  \lean{Module.Finite.of_localizationSpan_finite}
1269	  \mathlibok
1270	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.LocalProperties.Submodule}).}
1271	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(N\) an \(R\)-module, and \(t \subseteq R\) a
1272	  finite family with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = \top\) (the \(t\) generate the unit
1273	  ideal). Suppose that for each \(g \in t\) the localization \(N_g\) is a finite
1274	  module over the localization \(R_g\). Then \(N\) is a finite \(R\)-module.
1275	\end{lemma}
1276	
1277	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1278	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1279	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1281	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1282	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1283	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1284	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1285	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1286	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1287	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1288	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1289	\end{lemma}
1290	
1291	\begin{proof}
1292	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1293	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1294	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1295	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1296	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1297	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1298	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1299	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1300	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1301	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1302	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1303	\end{proof}

1320	  family with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) (so the basic opens \(D(g)\),
1321	  \(g \in t\), cover \(W\)), and that for each \(g \in t\) the section module
1322	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\). Then \(\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1323	  is a finite \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{proof}
1327	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen, lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer,
1328	        lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
1329	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (gluing step).}
1330	  By the local-on-a-spanning-family criterion \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan}
1331	  it suffices to show, for each \(g \in t\), that the localization of
1332	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at \(g\) is finite over the localization
1333	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). Fix \(g \in t\). The basic open \(D(g)\) is affine, and the
1334	  restriction \(\Gamma(X, W) \to \Gamma(X, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(X, D(g))\) as
1335	  the away-localization \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1336	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); moreover the section restriction
1337	  \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) as the
1338	  localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\)
1339	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). Thus \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) is a
1340	  model of the localized module, finite over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = (\Gamma(X, W))_g\)
1341	  by hypothesis; the model-transfer lemma
1342	  \cref{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer} carries this finiteness to the
1343	  canonical localized module of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) at the powers of \(g\), over
1344	  \((\Gamma(X, W))_g\). This supplies the per-element hypothesis, and
1345	  \cref{lem:module_finite_of_localizationSpan} concludes that \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is
1346	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1347	\end{proof}

1349	\subsection{Transport engine for generating sections}
1350	
1351	The restriction-of-generators lemma below is assembled from a generic transport
1352	principle: a finite generating family of a sheaf of modules \(\mathcal{M}\) pushes
1353	forward to a finite generating family of \(F(\mathcal{M})\) along any functor \(F\) of
1354	sheaves of modules that preserves colimits and carries the structure sheaf to the
1355	structure sheaf. The three blocks of this subsection package that principle (the
1356	generating family, its index type, and the finiteness bookkeeping). They are
1357	project-bespoke: Mathlib provides the analogous transport only for full presentations
1358	(with kernel data), not for bare generating families.
1359	
1360	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1361	[Transport of a generating family along a colimit-preserving functor]
1362	  \label{lem:genSections_map}
1363	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map}
1364	  Let \(F : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to \mathrm{Mod}\,S\) be a functor of sheaves of modules
1365	  that preserves colimits and is equipped with an isomorphism
1366	  \(\eta : F(\OO_R) \xrightarrow{\sim} \OO_S\) carrying the unit (structure sheaf) of the
1367	  source to that of the target. Given a generating family \(\sigma\) of \(\mathcal{M}\)
1368	  with index set \(I = \sigma.I\) and epimorphism
1369	  \(\sigma.\pi : \OO_R^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{M}\), the images
1370	  \((\mathrm{mapFree}\,F\,\eta\,I)^{-1} \circ F(\sigma.\pi)\) form a generating family of
1371	  \(F(\mathcal{M})\) with the same index set \(I\). The map is an epimorphism because it
1372	  is the composite of an isomorphism with \(F(\sigma.\pi)\), and \(F\) preserves the
1373	  epimorphism \(\sigma.\pi\).
1374	\end{lemma}
1375	
1376	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1377	[The transported family keeps its index type]
1378	  \label{lem:genSections_map_I}
1379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_I}
1380	  \uses{lem:genSections_map}
1381	  The index set of the transported generating family of \cref{lem:genSections_map} equals
1382	  the index set of the original: \((\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta)\!.I = \sigma.I\). This
1383	  holds definitionally.
1384	\end{lemma}
1385	
1386	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1387	[Finiteness survives transport]
1388	  \label{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1389	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.GeneratingSections.map_isFiniteType}
1390	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I}
1391	  If the original generating family \(\sigma\) is of finite type (its index set is finite),
1392	  then so is the transported family \(\mathrm{map}\,\sigma\,F\,\eta\): the index type is
1393	  unchanged by \cref{lem:genSections_map_I}, hence still finite.
1394	\end{lemma}
1395	
1396	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1397	[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict to a smaller open]
1398	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1399	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1400	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1401	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
1402	  Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) is
1403	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Then for any open \(V \le Y\) the
1404	  restricted family — the images of those generators under the section-restriction
1405	  \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) — is again a finite generating family of
1406	  \(\F|_V\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open (a family that generates
1407	  every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(V\)), and the index set, and hence
1408	  finiteness, is unchanged.
1409	\end{lemma}
1410	
1411	\begin{proof}
1412	  \uses{lem:genSections_map, lem:genSections_map_I, lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}
1413	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1414	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1415	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the inclusion \(V \le Y\) the
1416	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in V\) the stalk
1417	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1418	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1419	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus I} \to \F|_V\) is surjective on every stalk over \(V\), so
1420	  \(\{s_i|_V\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_V\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1421	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved. Concretely the restricted family is obtained by
1422	  transporting the generating family along the (colimit-preserving, unit-preserving)
1423	  restriction functors via the transport engine \cref{lem:genSections_map}, whose index
1424	  type is unchanged (\cref{lem:genSections_map_I}) and which preserves finiteness
1425	  (\cref{lem:genSections_map_isFiniteType}).
1426	\end{proof}
1427	
1428	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1429	[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1430	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1431	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1432	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1433	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1434	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1435	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1436	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1437	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1438	\end{lemma}
1439	
1440	\begin{proof}
1441	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1442	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1443	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1444	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1445	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1446	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1447	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1448	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1449	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1450	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1451	\end{proof}
1452	
1453	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1454	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1456	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a sheaf of modules on \(Y\). Then
1457	  \(\F\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1458	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1459	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F\). No quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is assumed:
1460	  the statement holds for arbitrary sheaves of modules on \(Y\), and applies in particular
1461	  to the restrictions \(\F|_{D(g)}\) arising in the cover assembly.
1462	\end{lemma}
1463	
1464	\begin{proof}
1465	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1466	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1467	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1468	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1469	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1470	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1471	  finite generating family.
1472	\end{proof}
1473	
1474	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1475	[Half 2, seam 1 — finite type yields a finite cover of globally-generated affines]
1476	  \label{lem:gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finiteType_affine_finite_cover_generated}
1478	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1479	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2101-L2107).
1480	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1481	  % "Assume $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module. This means
1482	  %  there exists an open covering of $X$ such that $\widetilde M$ restricted to
1483	  %  the members of this covering is globally generated by finitely many sections.
1484	  %  Thus there also exists a standard open covering
1485	  %  $X = \bigcup_{i = 1, \ldots, n} D(f_i)$ such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$
1486	  %  is generated by finitely many sections."
1487	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1488	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1489	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (standard
1490	  cover of generated affines).}
1491	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F\) a sheaf of modules of finite type,
1492	  and \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open. Then there is a finite family
1493	  \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that for
1494	  each \(g \in t\) the basic open \(D(g)\) is affine and \(\F|_{D(g)}\) is globally
1495	  generated by finitely many sections; equivalently, for each \(g \in t\) there is
1496	  a finite index set \(I_g\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g)}\)-modules
1497	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I_g} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g)}\).
1498	\end{lemma}
1499	
1500	\begin{proof}
1501	  \uses{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict, lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover,
1502	        lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1503	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (standard cover of generated affines).}
1504	  Finite type of \(\F\) means that \(X\) has an open covering \(\{Y_\alpha\}\) on each
1505	  member of which \(\F\) carries a finite generating family of sections. The intersections
1506	  \(\{Y_\alpha \cap W\}\) form an open cover of the affine \(W\). By the finite standard
1507	  subcover refinement \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} there is a finite family
1508	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1509	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) and each basic open \(D(g_i)\) contained in some
1510	  \(Y_\alpha \cap W\) — in particular \(D(g_i) \subseteq W\) is affine, and \(D(g_i)\)
1511	  embeds by an open immersion into the member \(Y_\alpha\) on which \(\F\) is finitely
1512	  generated. By the restriction-of-generators lemma
1513	  \cref{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict} the finite generating family on \(Y_\alpha\)
1514	  restricts to a finite generating family of \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\), so \(\F|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1515	  globally generated by finitely many sections. Finally, by the free-epimorphism
1516	  reformulation \cref{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}, this is equivalent to the existence
1517	  of a finite index set \(I_{g_i}\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_{D(g_i)}\)-modules
1518	  \(\OO_{D(g_i)}^{\oplus I_{g_i}} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_{D(g_i)}\). That the \(D(g_i)\)
1519	  cover \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) is exactly \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\). This is
1520	  Stacks' passage from the local-generation datum to a standard finite cover of generated
1521	  affines.
1522	\end{proof}
1523	
1524	\begin{lemma}[Affine $\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma$ adjunction with its $\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}$ counit]
1525	  \label{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib}
1526	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tilde.adjunction}
1527	  \mathlibok
1528	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}).}
1529	  On the affine scheme \(\Spec R\) the associated-sheaf functor
1530	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \mathrm{Mod}\,R \to (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules}\) is left adjoint to the
1531	  affine section functor \(\Gamma : (\Spec R).\mathrm{Modules} \to \mathrm{Mod}\,R\) (Mathlib's
1532	  \(\mathrm{moduleSpecΓFunctor}\), evaluation of a sheaf of modules at the top open). The counit of
1533	  this adjunction is the natural transformation
1534	  \(\Gamma(-)\,\widetilde{\phantom{x}} \to \mathrm{id}\) whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\)
1535	  is \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, \top)} \to \mathcal{M}\), and
1536	  the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful.
1537	\end{lemma}

1539	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1540	[Half 2, seam 2 — $\Gamma$ on affine quasi-coherent modules sends epis to surjections]
1541	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1542	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}
1543	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1544	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1545	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1546	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1547	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1548	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1549	  % epi over an affine.
1550	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1551	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1552	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1553	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1554	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1555	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1556	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1557	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1558	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1559	  \(B\)-module map.
1560	\end{lemma}
1561	
1562	\begin{proof}
1563	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1564	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1565	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1566	  affine).}
1567	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1568	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1569	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1570	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1571	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1572	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1573	
1574	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1575	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1576	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1577	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1578	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1579	  \[
1580	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1581	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1582	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1583	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1584	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1585	  \]
1586	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1587	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1588	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1589	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1590	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1591	
1592	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1593	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1594	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1595	\end{proof}

1650	\begin{proof}

1652	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB.}
1653	  This is the free-source specialisation of

1656	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\dashv\Gamma\) adjunction \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the unit
1657	  at \(N\) is an isomorphism \(N \xrightarrow{\sim} \Gamma(\widetilde{N}, V)\) (the left
1658	  adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is fully faithful), so \(\Gamma(\widetilde{N}, V)\) is a
1659	  finite \(R\)-module because \(N\) is; and the counit component at \(\widetilde{N}\),
1660	  namely \(\widetilde{N}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\), is an isomorphism (a fully-faithful left
1661	  adjoint has invertible counit on its image), supplying the source quasi-coherence
1662	  needed by seam 2. With both source hypotheses in hand,
1663	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_globally_generated} applied to
1664	  \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F\) yields that \(\Gamma(\F, V)\) is a finite
1665	  \(R\)-module.
1666	\end{proof}

1668	\begin{lemma}
1669	
1670	\leanok
1671	[Finiteness transports across a ring isomorphism and a semilinear additive map]
1672	  \label{lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1673	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1674	  Let \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\) be a ring isomorphism and
1675	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) an additive isomorphism that is \(\sigma\)-semilinear,
1676	  i.e. \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\) for all \(r \in R\), \(m \in M\). If
1677	  \(M\) is a finite \(R\)-module then \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.
1678	\end{lemma}
1679	
1680	\begin{proof}
1681	  Pick a finite \(R\)-spanning set \(T \subseteq M\). The image \(e(T)\) is finite, and
1682	  it \(R'\)-spans \(M'\): for \(m' \in M'\) write \(m' = e(m)\) with
1683	  \(m = \sum_i r_i t_i\) (\(t_i \in T\)); then
1684	  \(m' = e(\sum_i r_i t_i) = \sum_i \sigma(r_i)\, e(t_i)\) by semilinearity, an
1685	  \(R'\)-combination of \(e(T)\). Hence the top submodule of \(M'\) is finitely
1686	  generated, i.e. \(M'\) is a finite \(R'\)-module.
1687	\end{proof}

1689	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1690	[G1 base case — finite affine sections from a finite generating family]
1691	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1692	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_finite_sections_of_genSections}
1693	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1694	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear}
1695	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules, and
1696	  \(D \subseteq X\) an affine open. Suppose the restriction
1697	  \(\F|_D = (\mathrm{pullback}\,D.\iota)^{*}\F\) carries a finite generating family
1698	  \(\sigma\), with index set \(I = \sigma.I\). Then the section module
1699	  \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, D)\). This is the affine base
1700	  case of \(G1\): once the generators are in hand on an affine open, finiteness of
1701	  sections is purely an affine (Spec-side) statement. (Only quasi-coherence of \(\F\) is
1702	  needed — the finite-generation datum is carried by \(\sigma\) directly, so no separate
1703	  finite-type hypothesis on \(\F\) is required.)
1704	\end{lemma}
1705	
1706	\begin{proof}
1707	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi, lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
1708	        lem:module_finite_of_ringEquiv_semilinear, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1709	  The content is the transport of the finite generating datum
1710	  from the category of sheaves of modules over the affine open \(D\) to the affine
1711	  scheme \(\Spec \Gamma(X, D)\), where the affine base case applies. Write
1712	  \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\) and use the canonical identification
1713	  \(D \cong \Spec R\) (\(\mathrm{isoSpec}\)). The argument has three steps.
1714	
1715	  \emph{(a) Quasi-coherence on \(\Spec R\).} Transport \(\F|_D\) along
1716	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) to a sheaf of modules \(\F'\) on \(\Spec R\). Finite type of
1717	  \(\F\) is inherited by the restriction, and quasi-coherence of \(\F'\) is the assertion
1718	  that its counit \(\F'.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism; this is the affine
1719	  quasi-coherence criterion \cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ} fed by the basic-open
1720	  localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
1721	
1722	  \emph{(b) A finite free epimorphism over \(\Spec R\).} The generating family \(\sigma\)
1723	  furnishes an epimorphism \(\OO_D^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_D\). Transporting along
1724	  \(\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1}\) and using that the pullback of the free module along an
1725	  isomorphism is again free (\((\mathrm{pullback}\,\mathrm{isoSpec}^{-1})^{*}\OO_D^{\oplus I}
1726	  \cong \OO_{\Spec R}^{\oplus I}\)), together with the identification of the free sheaf with
1727	  the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\) of \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) (the associated-sheaf
1728	  functor preserves coproducts and \(\widetilde{R} \cong \OO_{\Spec R}\)), gives an
1729	  epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) with \(N = R^{\oplus I}\) a finite
1730	  \(R\)-module (\(I\) finite).
1731	
1732	  \emph{(c) Identification of section modules.} The affine global-sections functor sends
1733	  \(\F'\) to \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\), which the transport identifies with \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1734	  as \(R = \Gamma(X, D)\)-modules.
1735	
1736	  Now the affine base case \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_sections_free_epi}, applied to the finite-free
1737	  quasi-coherent epimorphism \(\widetilde{N} \twoheadrightarrow \F'\) over \(\Spec R\),
1738	  shows \(\Gamma(\F', \top)\) is a finite \(R\)-module; by (c) this is \(\Gamma(\F, D)\)
1739	  finite over \(\Gamma(X, D)\).
1740	\end{proof}

1063	\section{Graded-ring and graded-module assembly}
1064	\label{sec:sgr_graded_assembly}
1065	
1066	The components and multiplications of the previous two sections are assembled
1067	into the graded ring and graded module through Mathlib's
1068	\emph{external-direct-sum} graded API: the relevant inputs are families of
1069	abelian groups indexed by \(\mathbb{N}\) together with multiplication maps
1070	landing in the index-sum component, not submodules of a pre-existing ring.
1071	
1072	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded commutative semiring]
1073	  \label{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1074	  \lean{DirectSum.GCommSemiring}
1075	  \mathlibok
1076	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.DirectSum.Ring}).}
1077	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1078	  monoids equipped with a graded multiplication
1079	  \(A_i \times A_j \to A_{i+j}\) and a unit \(1 \in A_0\) satisfying graded
1080	  associativity, two-sided unitality, distributivity, and graded commutativity
1081	  (the data of a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\) on \(A\)). Then the external direct
1082	  sum \(\bigoplus_i A_i\) is a commutative semiring, with multiplication the
1083	  bilinear extension of the graded multiplication and identity the image of
1084	  \(1 \in A_0\).
1085	\end{lemma}
1086	
1087	\begin{lemma}[External-direct-sum graded module]
1088	  \label{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}
1089	  \lean{DirectSum.Gmodule}
1090	  \mathlibok
1091	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Module.GradedModule}).}
1092	  Let \(A : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) carry a graded semiring structure as
1093	  in \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib} and let
1094	  \(M : \mathbb{N} \to \mathrm{Type}\) be a family of additive commutative
1095	  monoids with a graded scalar action \(A_i \times M_j \to M_{i+j}\) satisfying
1096	  the graded module axioms (the data of a \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\) of \(M\) over
1097	  \(A\)). Then the external direct sum \(\bigoplus_j M_j\) is a module over the
1098	  semiring \(\bigoplus_i A_i\), with scalar multiplication the bilinear
1099	  extension of the graded action.
1100	\end{lemma}
1101	
1102	\begin{lemma}[Graded semiring structure on the section components]
1103	  \label{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1105	  \uses{def:sheafTensorPow, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1106	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib,
1107	    lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}
1108	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1109	  % preceding `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1110	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4257-L4267).
1111	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1112	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. We can define a $\mathbf{Z}$-graded
1113	  %  ring structure on $\bigoplus \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ by mapping
1114	  %  $s \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})$ and
1115	  %  $t \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes m})$ to the
1116	  %  section corresponding to $s \otimes t$ in
1117	  %  $\Gamma(X, \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n + m})$.
1118	  %  We omit the verification that this defines a commutative
1119	  %  and associative ring with $1$."
1120	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1121	  ring structure on \(\bigoplus \Gamma(X,\mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})\)).}
1122	  Let \(L_s\) be a line bundle on \(X_s\). The family of
1123	  \(\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})\)-modules
1124	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) carries a graded commutative
1125	  semiring structure (a \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\),
1126	  \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}) whose degree-\((m,m')\)
1127	  multiplication is the composite
1128	  \[
1129	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1130	      \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m'})
1131	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1132	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'}\bigr)
1133	    \xrightarrow{\ \Gamma(\mu_{m,m'})\ }
1134	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1135	  \]
1136	  the section multiplication (\cref{def:sectionMul}) followed by the
1137	  tensor-power comparison isomorphism (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}), with unit
1138	  \(1 \in \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{O}_{X_s}) = \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes 0})\)
1139	  (\cref{lem:moduleUnit_mathlib}). Consequently
1140	  \(R(X_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a
1141	  commutative semiring; this is the graded-ring structure underlying
1142	  \cref{def:sectionGradedRing}.
1143	\end{lemma}
1144	
1145	\begin{proof}
1146	  \uses{lem:sheafTensorPow_add, lem:sectionMul_coherent,
1147	    lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1148	  The graded multiplication maps are the composites displayed in the statement.
1149	  Graded associativity is the associativity coherence of
1150	  \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} combined with the associativity constraint of
1151	  the comparison isomorphisms \(\mu\) (\cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add}); graded
1152	  commutativity is the unit/commutativity of the section multiplication combined
1153	  with the commutativity constraint of \(\mu\); two-sided unitality is the unit
1154	  coherence of \cref{lem:sectionMul_coherent} together with
1155	  \(\mathcal{L}^{\otimes 0} = \mathcal{O}_X\). These are exactly the axioms of a
1156	  \(\mathrm{GCommSemiring}\), so \cref{lem:directSum_gcommSemiring_mathlib}
1157	  endows \(\bigoplus_{m \ge 0} \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m})\) with its
1158	  commutative-semiring structure.
1159	\end{proof}
