1161	\begin{lemma}[Graded module structure on the twisted-section components]
1162	  \label{lem:sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
1163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.sectionGradedModule_gmodule}
1164	  \uses{def:sheafModuleTwist, lem:sheafTensorPow_add, def:sectionMul,
1165	    lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib,
1166	    lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1167	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", \S "Invertible modules",
1168	  % `definition-gamma-star' (Tag 01CV) (read from
1169	  % references/stacks-modules.tex, L4279-L4286).
1170	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1171	  % "Given a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules $\mathcal{F}$ we set
1172	  %  $$
1173	  %  \Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L}, \mathcal{F})
1174	  %  =
1175	  %  \bigoplus\nolimits_{n \in \mathbf{Z}} \Gamma(X,
1176	  %  \mathcal{F} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{L}^{\otimes n})
1177	  %  $$
1178	  %  which we think of as a graded $\Gamma_*(X, \mathcal{L})$-module."
1179	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Sheaves of Modules", Tag 01CV (the graded
1180	  module \(\Gamma_*(X,\mathcal{L},\mathcal{F})\)).}
1181	  Let \(\mathcal{F}_s\) be a coherent sheaf and \(L_s\) a line bundle on
1182	  \(X_s\). The family
1183	  \(m \mapsto \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\)
1184	  (\cref{def:sheafModuleTwist}) carries a graded module structure (a
1185	  \(\mathrm{Gmodule}\), \cref{lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib}) over the graded
1186	  semiring of \cref{lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}, with degree-\((m,m')\)
1187	  action
1188	  \[
1189	    \Gamma(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m}) \otimes_{\Gamma(X_s,\mathcal{O}_{X_s})}
1190	      \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})
1191	    \xrightarrow{\ \cdot\ }
1192	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, L_s^{\otimes m} \otimes
1193	      (\mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m'})\bigr)
1194	    \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }
1195	    \Gamma\bigl(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes (m+m')}\bigr),
1196	  \]
1197	  the section multiplication followed by the comparison isomorphism that
1198	  reassociates and merges the line-bundle factors (using
1199	  \cref{lem:sheafTensorPow_add} and the symmetry of the tensor product).
1200	  Consequently \(M(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s, L_s) = \bigoplus_{m \ge 0}
1201	  \Gamma(X_s, \mathcal{F}_s \otimes L_s^{\otimes m})\) is a graded module over
1202	  \(R(X_s, L_s)\); this is the graded-module structure underlying
1203	  \cref{def:sectionGradedModule}.
1204	\end{lemma}

1206	\begin{proof}
1207	  \uses{lem:sectionMul_coherent, lem:sheafTensorPow_add,
1208	    lem:directSum_gmodule_mathlib, lem:sectionGradedRing_gcommSemiring}
1209	  The action maps are the displayed composites. The graded-module axioms ---

1211	  (\(r \cdot (r' \cdot x) = (r r') \cdot x\)) and unitality
1212	  (\(1 \cdot x = x\)) --- follow from the associativity and unit coherences of

954	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
955	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
956	  % "represents the
957	  %  contravariant functor
958	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
959	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
960	  %  set of all equivalence classes
961	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
962	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
963	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
964	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
965	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
966	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
967	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
968	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
969	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
970	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
971	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
972	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
973	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
974	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
975	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
976	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
977	  \[
978	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
979	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
980	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
981	  \]
982	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
983	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
984	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
985	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
986	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
987	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
988	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
989	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
990	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
991	\end{definition}

993	\section{The universal property}
994	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
995	
996	\begin{theorem}
997	
998	\leanok
999	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
1000	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
1001	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
1002	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
1003	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
1004	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
1005	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1006	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
1007	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
1008	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1009	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
1010	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
1011	  %  contravariant functor
1012	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
1013	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
1014	  %  set of all equivalence classes
1015	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
1016	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
1017	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
1018	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
1019	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
1020	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
1021	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
1022	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
1023	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
1024	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
1025	  \[
1026	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
1027	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
1028	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
1029	    \qquad
1030	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
1031	      \;\longmapsto\;
1032	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
1033	  \]
1034	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
1035	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1036	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
1037	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
1038	  universal family.
1039	\end{theorem}
1040	
1041	\begin{proof}
1042	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
1043	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
1044	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
1045	    def:grassmannian_functor}
1046	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
1047	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
1048	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
1049	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
1050	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
1051	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
1052	
1053	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
1054	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
1055	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
1056	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
1057	  the composite
1058	  \[
1059	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
1060	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
1061	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
1062	  \]
1063	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
1064	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
1065	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
1066	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
1067	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
1068	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
1069	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
1070	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
1071	
1072	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
1073	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
1074	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
1075	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
1076	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
1077	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
1078	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
1079	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
1080	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
1081	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
1082	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
1083	
1084	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
1085	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
1086	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
1087	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
1088	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
1089	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
1090	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
1091	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
1092	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
1093	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
1094	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
1095	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
1096	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
1097	
1098	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
1099	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
1100	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
1101	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
1102	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
1103	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
1104	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
1105	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
1106	
1107	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
1108	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
1109	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
1110	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
1111	\end{proof}

1910	  Let \(R \to B\) be a ring map, \(T \subseteq B\) a submonoid, \(N\) a \(B\)-module,
1911	  and \(K\) an \(R\)-module. There is an isomorphism, natural in \(K\),
1912	  \[

1914	      \;\cong\; (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K .

1919	  naturally in the \(B\)-module \(M\). Applying this to \(M = N \otimes_R K\) and using
1920	  associativity/commutativity of the tensor product over the tower
1921	  \(R \to B \to T^{-1}B\) gives
1922	  \(T^{-1}B \otimes_B (N \otimes_R K) \cong (T^{-1}B \otimes_B N) \otimes_R K
1923	  \cong (\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,T\,N) \otimes_R K\); naturality in \(K\) is inherited
1924	  from the tensor functor.

2010	  pair \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\), \(\bar g \in B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\) cutting out the same basic open

2012	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}). Then the single group \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) carries two
2013	  localized-module structures:
2014	  \begin{enumerate}
2015	    \item the section restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2016	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W)\); and
2017	    \item the section restriction \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) as
2018	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g)^{-1}\,M_j = (M_j)_{\bar g}\).
2019	  \end{enumerate}
2020	  Consequently \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\) canonically, by
2021	  uniqueness of localized modules.
2022	\end{lemma}
2023	
2024	\begin{proof}
2025	  \uses{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq, lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen,
2026	        lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
2027	  By \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq} the two recipes for the open agree,
2028	  \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\), so \(\Gamma(\F, D)\) is one and the
2029	  same group whether \(D\) is read in chart \(W\) or in chart \(W_j\). Applying the quasi-coherent
2030	  section-localization datum \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} with ambient affine \(W\)
2031	  and element \(g\), the restriction \(\Gamma(\F, W) \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at
2032	  \(\mathrm{powers}\,g\), giving (1). Applying the same datum with ambient affine \(W_j\) and element
2033	  \(\bar g\) (legitimate since \(D = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g \le W_j\)), the restriction
2034	  \(M_j \to \Gamma(\F, D)\) is the localization at \(\mathrm{powers}\,\bar g\), giving (2). Both maps
2035	  localize their respective sources onto the same target \(\Gamma(\F, D)\); the universal property of
2036	  localized modules (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) uniqueness) supplies the canonical isomorphism
2037	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,g)^{-1}\,\Gamma(\F, W) \cong (M_j)_{\bar g}\). Finiteness of \(M_j\) over \(B_j\)
2038	  is recorded by \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}.
2039	\end{proof}

2041	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2042	[B2.3 — base comparison: \(\Gamma(S,U)\) localizes \(A_f\)]
2043	  \label{lem:gf_base_localization_comparison}
2044	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_base_localization_comparison}
2045	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2046	  % NOTE (iter-054): the Lean proves the strictly WEAKER form `Module.Flat Γ(S,V) Γ(S,U)`
2047	  % (flatness of the restriction algebra), NOT `IsLocalization` as the prose states. Flatness
2048	  % is all the assembly consumes (localizations are flat ⟹ this suffices), and it is reached
2049	  % directly via `Flat.flat_appLE (𝟙 S)`. Planner: downgrade the prose claim to flatness, or
2050	  % strengthen the Lean to `IsLocalization`, to remove the name-vs-type mismatch.
2051	  In the situation of the generic-flatness assembly \cref{thm:generic_flatness}, let
2052	  \(U \le V = D(f) \subseteq U_0 = \Spec A\)
2053	  be an affine open of \(S\). Then \(A_f = \Gamma(S, D(f))\) is the away-localization of \(A\) at
2054	  \(f\), and the restriction \(A_f \to R := \Gamma(S, U)\) realises \(R\) as a localization of \(A_f\)
2055	  (both are rings of fractions of the domain \(A\) inside its fraction field, \(R\) inverting at least
2056	  the denominators already inverted in \(A_f\)).
2057	\end{lemma}

2059	\begin{proof}

2062	  \(A = \Gamma(S, U_0) \to \Gamma(S, D(f))\) exhibits \(A_f = \Gamma(S, D(f))\) as the
2063	  away-localization of \(A\) at \(f\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}). The affine open
2064	  \(U \le D(f) = \Spec A_f\) of the integral scheme \(S\) has section ring
2065	  \(R = \Gamma(S, U) \subseteq \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) containing \(A_f\); the comparison
2066	  \(A_f \to R\) is the restriction along \(U \le D(f)\) and exhibits \(R\) as a localization of
2067	  \(A_f\) inside the fraction field of the domain \(A\).
2068	\end{proof}

2070	\subsection{Base descent along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\)}
2071	\label{sec:gf_base_descent_isEpi}
2072	
2073	The generic-flatness construction yields, on each patch, flatness of the section module
2074	over the base ring \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) of the witness open \(V = D(f)\). For an
2075	\emph{arbitrary} affine open \(U \le V\), the base ring \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\) need not be a
2076	localization of \(A_f\), so the localization-descent of
2077	\cref{lem:gf_base_localization_comparison} does not transport flatness directly. The correct
2078	mechanism is that the inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow V\) of affine opens is an \emph{open
2079	immersion}, hence a monomorphism of schemes, so the restriction ring map
2080	\(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\) is a ring \emph{epimorphism}. For a ring epimorphism
2081	\(A \to R\) the canonical base-change map \(R \otimes_A M \to M\) is an isomorphism, and
2082	flatness descends along it. This replaces the earlier \texttt{IsBaseChange} hypothesis (once
2083	flagged as absent from Mathlib) by the verified Mathlib epimorphism API.
2084	
2085	\begin{definition}[Mathlib anchor — ring-epimorphism typeclass]
2086	  \label{lem:mathlib_algebra_isEpi}
2087	  \lean{Algebra.IsEpi}
2088	  \mathlibok
2089	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Algebra.Epi}).}
2090	  For a commutative ring \(R\) and an \(R\)-algebra \(A\), the predicate
2091	  \(\mathrm{Algebra.IsEpi}\,R\,A\) asserts that the structure map \(R \to A\) is an
2092	  epimorphism in the category of commutative rings (equivalently, the multiplication map
2093	  \(A \otimes_R A \to A\) is an isomorphism).
2094	\end{definition}
2095

2100	  \uses{lem:mathlib_algebra_isEpi}

2103	  \(A\)-module regarded as an \(R\)-module through the scalar tower \(R \to A \to M\). Then
2104	  the canonical map exhibits the base change \(A \otimes_R M\) as \(M\) itself, an
2105	  isomorphism of \(A\)-modules
2106	  \[
2107	    \mathrm{TensorProduct.lid'} : A \otimes_R M \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; M .

2183	\begin{lemma}[Restriction between affine opens is a ring epimorphism]
2184	  \label{lem:gf_openImmersion_isEpi}

2188	        lem:mathlib_algebra_isEpi}
2189	  Let \(U \le V\) be affine opens of a scheme \(S\). Then the structure-sheaf restriction
2190	  \(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\) is a ring epimorphism, i.e.\
2191	  \(\mathrm{Algebra.IsEpi}\,\Gamma(S, V)\,\Gamma(S, U)\).

2198	  schemes, hence a monomorphism (\cref{lem:mathlib_isOpenImmersion_mono}). Both \(U\) and
2199	  \(V\) are affine, so under the canonical identifications \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(S, U)\)
2200	  and \(V \cong \Spec \Gamma(S, V)\) (\cref{lem:mathlib_isAffineOpen_isoSpec}) the

2203	  (\cref{lem:mathlib_spec_fullyFaithful}), a monomorphism between affine schemes
2204	  corresponds to an \emph{epimorphism} of section rings; thus
2205	  \(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{CommRingCat}\). Finally
2206	  \cref{lem:mathlib_commRingCat_epi_iff_epi} repackages this categorical epimorphism as the
2207	  ring-epimorphism property \(\mathrm{Algebra.IsEpi}\,\Gamma(S, V)\,\Gamma(S, U)\).

2284	  \(x \in D(g)\), \(D(g) \le W \sqcap W_i\), \(D(\bar g) \le W \sqcap W_i\), and a common basic
2285	  open \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\). The opens simultaneously basic
2286	  in \(W\) and in \(W_i\) thus form a basis of the overlap.

2292	  (\(\texttt{IsAffineOpen.exists\_basicOpen\_le}\) in chart \(W_i\)), then a basic open \(D(g)\) of
2293	  \(W\) with \(x \in D(g) \subseteq D(b)\) (in chart \(W\)); hence \(D(g) \le W \sqcap W_i\).

2295	  \(\Gamma(X, D(b)) \cong (\Gamma(X, W_i))_b\) (\(\texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_basicOpen}\)),
2296	  so write \(g|_{D(b)} = \bar g'/b^{n}\) by \(\texttt{IsLocalization.surj}\) and set
2297	  \(\bar g := b \cdot \bar g'\). Then \(X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g = D(b) \sqcap X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g' = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,g\)
2298	  (\(\texttt{Scheme.basicOpen\_mul}\) plus \(\texttt{basicOpen\_res}\) bookkeeping, since \(b\) is a
2299	  unit on \(D(b)\)), which is the basic-open equality. Note (iter-054 finding): only the basic-open
2300	  equality is achievable in general; a restriction-matched pair \(g|_O = \bar g|_O\) need not exist.

2308	  % NOTE (iter-054): the "matched pair … that agree over the overlap" (restriction-match
2309	  % g|_O = ḡ|_O) is OVER-SPECIFIED — the Lean conclusion delivers ONLY the basic-open equality

2315	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(W \subseteq X\) an affine open, and \(\{W_j\}_{j}\) a finite family of
2316	  affine opens of \(X\) whose union contains \(W\). Then there is a finite index set \(K\) and, for
2317	  each \(k \in K\), a chart index \(j(k)\) together with sections \(g_k \in \Gamma(X, W)\),
2318	  \(\bar g_k \in \Gamma(X, W_{j(k)})\) cutting out a common basic open
2319	  \[
2320	    D(g_k) \;=\; X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g_k \;\le\; W \sqcap W_{j(k)},
2321	  \]

2323	  \(D(g_k)\) cover \(W\)). This is the spanning family fed to

2329	  Inside each overlap \(W \cap W_j\) — open in both affines \(W\) and \(W_j\) — the opens that are
2330	  simultaneously basic in \(W\) and basic in \(W_j\) form a basis of the subspace topology: given
2331	  \(x \in W \cap W_j\), choose \(b \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) with \(x \in D(b) \subseteq W \cap W_j\) and
2332	  then \(g \in \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(x \in D(g) \subseteq D(b)\); shrinking \(b\) to the common open
2333	  and recording its restriction over the overlap produces a basic open of \(W\) that is matched, over
2334	  \(W \cap W_j\), by a section \(\bar g \in \Gamma(X, W_j)\) (\cref{lem:gf_crossChart_basicOpen_eq}
2335	  certifies \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\)). Ranging over all \(j\), and because the \(W_j\)
2336	  cover \(W\), these common basic opens form an open cover of the affine \(W\). The affine \(W\) is
2337	  quasi-compact, so a finite subfamily already covers it: applying the standard-subcover extraction
2338	  \cref{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover} to this open cover of \(W\) returns a finite family
2339	  \(\{g_k\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g_k\} = \top\), each \(D(g_k)\) a
2340	  common basic open contained in some \(W \cap W_{j(k)}\) with matching
2341	  \(\bar g_k \in \Gamma(X, W_{j(k)})\).
2342	\end{proof}
2343	
2344	
2345	
2346	\begin{theorem}
2347	
2348	
2349	
2350	\leanok
2351	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
2352	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
2353	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
2354	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
2355	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
2356	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
2357	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2358	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
2359	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
2360	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
2361	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
2362	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
2363	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
2364	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
2365	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
2366	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
2367	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
2368	
2369	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
2370	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
2371	\end{theorem}
2372	
2373	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
2374	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
2375	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
2376	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
2377	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
2378	% by restricting to a non-empty
2379	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
2380	\begin{proof}
2381	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
2382	        lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover, lem:gf_section_span_flat_descent,
2383	        lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg, lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat,
2384	        lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase, lem:gf_openImmersion_isEpi,
2385	        lem:gf_flat_descend_isEpi, lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span,
2386	        lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}
2387	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
2388	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
2389	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
2390	
2391	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact and
2392	  \(U_0\) is affine, hence quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is
2393	  quasi-compact; choose a
2394	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
2395	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
2396	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
2397	
2398	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
2399	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
2400	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
2401	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
2402	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
2403	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
2404	
2405	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
2406	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
2407	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
2408	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
2409	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
2410	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
2411	
2412	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness (source-span descent with base descent along
2413	  \(U \hookrightarrow V\)).} On each patch the localized section module \((M_j)_f\) is free,
2414	  hence flat over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\). We promote these per-patch freeness statements to
2415	  the flatness conclusion for every affine \(U \le V\) and affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), with
2416	  \(R := \Gamma(S, U)\), by a \emph{source-span} descent on \(W\).
2417	
2418	  Cover \(W\) by basic opens aligned to the patches: by the patch-aligned covering lemma
2419	  \cref{lem:gf_crossChart_spanning_cover} there is a finite family
2420	  \(\{g\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,\{g\} = \top\), each
2421	  \(D(g) \subseteq W \cap W_{j}\) a common basic open matched by a patch element
2422	  \(\bar g \in B_{j}\) (so \(D(g) = X.\mathrm{basicOpen}\,\bar g\)). For each such \(g\): the
2423	  two-leg transport \cref{lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg} identifies the section module
2424	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) \cong (M_{j})_{\bar g}\). The per-patch freeness gives \((M_{j})_f\)
2425	  free, hence flat over \(A_f\) (\cref{lem:gf_patch_free_imp_flat},
2426	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_free}); since \(D(g)\) lies over \(U \le V = D(f)\) the image of
2427	  \(f\) is already invertible on \(D(\bar g)\), so localizing \((M_{j})_f\) at the
2428	  powers of \(\bar g\) keeps \(A_f\)-flatness
2429	  (\cref{lem:gf_flat_localizedModule_sameBase}) and yields exactly \(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat
2430	  over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\).
2431	
2432	  \emph{Base descent along the open immersion.} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow V\) of
2433	  affine opens makes \(\Gamma(S, V) \to \Gamma(S, U)\) a ring epimorphism
2434	  (\cref{lem:gf_openImmersion_isEpi}); flat descent along a ring epimorphism
2435	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_descend_isEpi} therefore upgrades ``\(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat over
2436	  \(\Gamma(S, V)\)'' to ``\(\Gamma(\F, D(g))\) flat over \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\)''. Finally the
2437	  \(\{g\}\) span the unit ideal of \(\Gamma(X, W)\), so the source-span flatness criterion
2438	  \cref{lem:mathlib_flat_of_isLocalized_span}, packaged as
2439	  \cref{lem:gf_section_span_flat_descent}, descends these flat principal localizations to
2440	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) flat over \(R = \Gamma(S, U)\). This holds for every affine \(U \le V\)
2441	  and \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\), producing the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over
2442	  \(\OO_V\).
2443	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Quot scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/QuotScheme.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GradedHilbertSerre.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter packages the Quot-foundations layer of the
6	% Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman Quot-scheme construction
7	% \(\mathrm{Quot}^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\): the per-fiber Hilbert polynomial, the
8	% Quot functor of \(T\)-flat coherent quotients of \(E_T\) on
9	% \(X_T = X \times_S T\), and the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} together with
10	% its representability. The Quot functor underlies the relative Picard
11	% functor via the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
12	% -- the case \(E = \mathcal{O}_X\) of the Quot functor.
13	%
14	% LOAD-BEARING SUB-PREREQUISITE. The Quot construction embeds Quot as a
15	% locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian
16	% \(\mathrm{Grass}(W \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_S} \mathrm{Sym}^r V, \Phi(r))\).
17	% Mathlib (at the pinned commit) carries no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} -- only
18	% a linear-algebra Grassmannian as a finite-rank-quotient variety. The
19	% scheme-level Grassmannian over an arbitrary noetherian base is therefore an
20	% in-project sub-build. This chapter introduces it as
21	% \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} and states its representability
22	% (\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:quot_setup}
26	
27	Throughout this chapter, let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a
28	projective morphism, \(L\) a relatively very ample line bundle on \(X\) over \(S\),
29	and \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\). The Quot construction packages the
30	\(T\)-flat coherent quotients \(E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of the
31	pull-back \(E_T\) to \(X_T = X \times_S T\), modulo the equivalence
32	\(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}', q'\rangle\) defined by
33	\(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). Fixing the Hilbert polynomial
34	\(\Phi(\lambda) = \chi(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}(\lambda)) \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\)
35	(constant on connected \(T\) by flatness)
36	stratifies the Quot functor into pieces \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\), each of
37	which Grothendieck proved is representable by a projective \(S\)-scheme.
38	This chapter develops the foundations of that picture: the Hilbert
39	polynomial (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}), the Quot functor
40	(\cref{def:quot_functor}), and the Grassmannian scheme
41	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) with its representability
42	(\cref{thm:grassmannian_representable}).
43	
44	% SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1;
45	% FGA Explained Ch.~5, AMS MSM 123 (read from
46	% references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L287-L500).
47	% The Hartshorne reference for the polynomial-eventually
48	% property of Euler characteristics is [Hartshorne], III.5.2 (the scanned
49	% PDF at references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf has no text layer, so
50	% the verbatim source-quote is taken from
51	% Nitsure \S 1; Hartshorne III.5.2 is cited in prose as the textbook
52	% reference).
53	
54	\section{Hilbert polynomial of a coherent sheaf}
55	\label{sec:hilbert_polynomial}
56	
57	\begin{definition}\leanok
58	[Hilbert polynomial]
59	  \label{def:hilbert_polynomial}
60	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.hilbertPolynomial}
61	  \uses{thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule}
62	  % ENCODING. `def:hilbert_polynomial` is formalized via the GRADED HILBERT
63	  % FUNCTION of the section module ⊕_{m≥0} Γ(X_s, F_s ⊗ L_s^{⊗m})
64	  % (def:sectionGradedModule), whose polynomiality is extracted in
65	  % thm:hilbertPoly_of_sectionModule from the graded Hilbert–Serre rationality
66	  % lemma (lem:gradedHilbertSerre_rational) together with the Mathlib lemma
67	  % Polynomial.existsUnique_hilbertPoly (lem:hilbertPoly_exists_mathlib). The
68	  % construction uses only H^0; for m≫0 it agrees with the Euler characteristic
69	  % χ by Serre vanishing, so it is the classical Hilbert polynomial and no
70	  % numerical invariant is lost.

1	\chapter{Relative Spec}
2	\label{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-172). This chapter is the foundational A.1.a sub-row of
6	% Route A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,
10	% AffineScheme.glueOpens) but no general RelativeSpec construction; this chapter is
11	% the prover-ready specification for the new file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean
12	% which iter-173 (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its core properties --- universal property and affine
26	base --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure
64	%     `structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where`
65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

Now I understand. The statement block (lines 673-702) has `\uses{}` at lines 676-680 with 6 items. The proof block (lines 704-...) has `\uses{}` at lines 705-710 with 8 items (6 old + 2 new). 

The leandag node's `uses` = 6 items (from the statement \uses{}). The proof-block 2 new items (`lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso, def:relTensorDomainPresheaf`) are NOT in the node's `uses` array because... 

So the node's `uses` array contains exactly those 6 items from the statement block's `\uses{}`.  The proof block adds 2 more items, but those aren't being merged into the node's uses. This makes me wonder if leandag has different rules depending on whether a statement block `\uses{}` exists — if it does, use that; if it doesn't, fall back to the proof block's `\uses{}`. That would explain why `lem:gf_section_localization_twoleg` pulls from the proof block while `lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit` only uses the statement block.

Let me verify this pattern by checking a node that has both statement and proof `\uses{}` to see what actually gets included in the node's uses array. So the pattern is: when a statement has `\uses{}`, that takes precedence and the proof's `\uses{}` is ignored entirely. Only when there's no statement `\uses{}` does the proof's `\uses{}` get used. This aligns with standard leanblueprint v1 behavior where the theorem body's `\uses{}` is what matters.

Now I'm realizing the actual distinction: statement `\uses{}` creates the DAG edges that determine readiness ordering, while proof `\uses{}` is just metadata stored in the proof tex without affecting the dependency graph. So for `lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit`, those 6 items in the statement `\uses{}` are what create the ordering constraints, and the 2 extra items in the proof `\uses{}` don't propagate as dependencies.

The critical issue is that `lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso` and `def:relTensorDomainPresheaf` are only in the proof `\uses{}`, not the statement `\uses{}`, which means they have no DAG edges pointing to them and won't be automatically prioritized for proving. I need to move these two items from the proof `\uses{}` into the statement `\uses{}` of `lem:isIso_sheafification_whiskerRight_unit` to fix the dependency ordering. The dependency ordering doesn't actually include these as prerequisites for the main lemma. Both `lem:snap_ztensor_whisker_localIso` and `def:relTensorDomainPresheaf` are missing critical pieces: the first needs a `\lean{}` hint to be provable, and the second needs to be marked as leanok. I need to add the `\lean{}` hint to the first lemma and move both into the statement-level `\uses{}` of the main lemma so they're recognized as dependencies.

Looking at the uses declarations, the statement block lists two items but the proof only references one, creating a mismatch. The blueprint flagged that `lem:localIso_toPresheaf_map_unit` in the uses might be incorrect since the proof doesn't actually invoke it. This is a spurious dependency issue that's less critical but still worth noting. Now I'm looking at the isolated nodes in the Flattening Stratification chapter—there are four candidates with zero dependencies and zero reverse dependencies that I need to investigate further to see if they're remnants from an earlier version of the proof structure.

python3 -c "
import json
with open('.leandag/dag.json') as f:
    dag = json.load(f)

nodes_raw = dag['nodes']
nodes = {n['id']: n for n in nodes_raw}

# Check the isolated nodes
isolated_ids = ['lem:mathlib_flat_localization_preserves', 'lem:mathlib_localization_flat', 
                'lem:mathlib_flat_of_localized_maximal', 'lem:mathlib_flat_trans',
                'lem:gr_homEquiv_conjugateEquiv_app']

for nid in isolated_ids:
    n = nodes.get(nid, {})
    if n:
        print(f'{nid}:')
        print(f'  chapter: {n.get(\"chapter\",\"?\")[:60]}')
        print(f'  lean_name: {n.get(\"lean_name\")}')
        print(f'  mathlib_ok: {n.get(\"mathlib_ok\")}')
        print(f'  proved: {n.get(\"proved\")}')
        print(f'  dep_count={n.get(\"dep_count\",0)}, rdep_count={n.get(\"rdep_count\",0)}')
        stmt = n.get('statement', '')[:150]
        print(f'  statement: {stmt!r}')
        print()
" 2>&1

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to

1	\chapter{Regrouping isomorphism for the affine base-change tensor tower}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_RegroupHelper}
3	% This chapter blueprints the standalone, geometry-free helper that lives in
4	% AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/RegroupHelper.lean (1:1 slug). It is the pure
5	% tensor-algebra core of lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv in the FlatBaseChange
6	% chapter; it is split into its own compilation unit so the object-level
7	% ModuleCat iso can be obtained by a single LinearEquiv.toModuleIso application
8	% (the separate import normalises the `Module A (A ⊗[R] R')` instance diamond
9	% that blocks the same-file construction).
10	
11	\section{The R'-linear regrouping equivalence}
12	
13	\begin{lemma}
14	
15	\leanok
16	[Regrouping linear equivalence for the tensor tower]
17	  \label{lem:base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_regroup_linearEquiv}
19	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
20	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
21	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
22	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
23	  % L933--938).
24	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
25	  % equality
26	  % $$
27	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
28	  % $$
29	  % as $R'$-modules."
30	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
31	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
32	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\)
33	  an \(A\)-module. On the \emph{canonical} Mathlib instances (with \(R'\) acting on
34	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) through the \(\texttt{Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra}\)
35	  factor, i.e.\ \(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)) there is a bundled
36	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
37	  \[
38	    \operatorname{regroup} :
39	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
40	  \]
41	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the affine base-change source. On generators
42	  it sends \((a \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes (a \cdot m)\); in
43	  particular \((1 \otimes r') \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\), with inverse
44	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
45	  hypothesis.
46	\end{lemma}
47	
48	\begin{proof}
49	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
50	  \leanok
51	  This is pure tensor algebra, and it is supplied \emph{directly} by Mathlib's
52	  pushout-form base-change cancellation
53	  \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange}\)
54	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}), instantiated at \(S = R'\),
55	  \(B = A \otimes_R R'\):
56	  \[
57	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} R\,R'\,A\,(A \otimes_R R')\,M :
58	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M .
59	  \]
60	  The required pushout instance \(\operatorname{Algebra.IsPushout} R\,R'\,A\,
61	  (A \otimes_R R')\) is supplied for free by \(\operatorname{TensorProduct.isPushout'}\).
62	  The decisive structural point is that this equivalence is \emph{natively}
63	  \(R'\)-linear: the cancellation is bundled as a \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over
64	  \(R'\) (its base-ring slot is \(S = R'\)), so the \(R'\)-linearity is part of the
65	  Mathlib datum with no hand-written scalar-compatibility obligation.
66	  On the generator it computes, by \(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_tmul}\),
67	  \[
68	    (a \otimes r') \otimes m \;\longmapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m) ,
69	  \]
70	  with inverse \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\)
71	  (\(\texttt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)); the \(R'\)-action enters through the
72	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) factor (\(r' \cdot (a \otimes s) = a \otimes (r' s)\)). Being a
73	  single bundled Mathlib linear equivalence it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and it
74	  carries no flatness hypothesis.
75	
76	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	
4	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
5	\label{sec:gr_setup}
6	
7	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
8	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
9	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
10	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
11	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
12	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
13	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
14	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
15	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
16	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
17	
18	\begin{definition}
19	
20	\leanok
21	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
22	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
23	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
24	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
25	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
26	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
27	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
28	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
29	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
30	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
31	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
32	  %  $I$.
33	  %
34	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
35	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
36	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
37	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
38	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
39	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
40	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
43	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
44	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
45	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
46	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
47	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
48	  set
49	  \[
50	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
51	  \]
52	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
53	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
54	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
55	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
56	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
57	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
58	\end{definition}
59	
60	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
61	\label{sec:gr_transition}
62	
63	\subsection*{Mathlib inputs for the transition map}
64	
65	The construction of \(\theta_{I,J}\) is a Cramer-inverse computation over the
66	localised chart ring, followed by one application of the universal property of a
67	localisation. We record the four Mathlib facts it rests on as dependency
68	anchors.
69	
70	\begin{lemma}
71	[Localised element is a unit]
72	  \label{lem:mathlib_away_algebraMap_isUnit}
73	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.algebraMap_isUnit}
74	  \mathlibok
75	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
76	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(x \in R\), and \(S\) a localisation of \(R\)
77	  away from \(x\) (i.e.\ \(S = R[1/x]\)). Then the image of \(x\) under the
78	  structure map \(R \to S\) is a unit of \(S\).
79	\end{lemma}

import json

chapter_nodes = defaultdict(list)

    chapter_nodes[ch].append(n)

# Print summary per chapter
for ch, nodes in sorted(chapter_nodes.items()):

    mathlib = sum(1 for n in nodes if n.get('mathlib_ok'))
    unproved = sum(1 for n in nodes if not n.get('proved') and not n.get('mathlib_ok'))
    total = len(nodes)
    print(f'{ch[:55]!r}:')
    print(f'  total={total}, proved_clean={proved}, has_sorry={sorry}, mathlib={mathlib}, unproved={unproved}')
" 2>&1

python3 -c "
import json
with open('.leandag/dag.json') as f:
    dag = json.load(f)

nodes_raw = dag['nodes']

chapters = {
    'Flat': 'Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coheren',
    'GF': 'Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf',
    'SNAP': 'Section graded ring infrastructure: tensor powers and g',
    'GrQuot': 'The tautological quotient and the universal property of',
}

for key, ch_prefix in chapters.items():
    nodes = [n for n in nodes_raw if n.get('chapter','').startswith(ch_prefix)]
    print(f'=== {key} ({ch_prefix[:40]}) ===')
    unproved = [n for n in nodes if not n.get('proved') and not n.get('mathlib_ok')]
    for n in unproved:
        print(f'  UNPROVED: {n[\"id\"]} lean={n.get(\"lean_name\")!r} dep={n.get(\"dep_count\",0)} rdep={n.get(\"rdep_count\",0)}')
    sorry = [n for n in nodes if n.get('has_sorry')]
    for n in sorry:
        print(f'  SORRY: {n[\"id\"]} lean={n.get(\"lean_name\")!r}')
    print()
" 2>&1

/-! ### Pullback of free sheaves along an arbitrary scheme morphism

The functor of points (`AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor`) acts on morphisms by
pullback, and for that one needs `f^* (O^n) ≅ O^n` for an *arbitrary* scheme morphism
`f`. Mathlib's `SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` supplies this only when the underlying
site functor `Opens.map f.base` is `Final`; the previous chapters discharged that `Final`
hypothesis only for open immersions and isomorphisms. The first lemma below removes the
restriction entirely: `Opens.map f.base` is `Final` for *every* scheme morphism, because
the structured-arrow category over any open `V` has a terminal object (the whole space
`⊤`). With that in hand, `pullbackFreeIso` and `pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank` hold for all
morphisms. -/

/-- For an arbitrary scheme morphism `φ`, the site functor `Opens.map φ.base` is `Final`:
over any open `V` of the target the structured-arrow category `{U : V ≤ φ⁻¹ U}` has the
terminal object `U = ⊤`, hence is connected. This is the missing ingredient that makes
`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjUnitToUnit`/`pullbackObjFreeIso` applicable to *every* morphism,
not just to open immersions. Project-local. -/
lemma opensMap_final {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) :
    (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base).Final := by
  constructor
  intro V
  set top : StructuredArrow V (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base) :=
    StructuredArrow.mk (Y := (⊤ : T.Opens)) (homOfLE le_top)
  haveI : Nonempty (StructuredArrow V (TopologicalSpace.Opens.map φ.base)) := ⟨top⟩
  apply zigzag_isConnected
  intro s t
  have hs : s ⟶ top := StructuredArrow.homMk (homOfLE le_top) (Subsingleton.elim _ _)
  have ht : t ⟶ top := StructuredArrow.homMk (homOfLE le_top) (Subsingleton.elim _ _)
  exact Relation.ReflTransGen.trans
    (Relation.ReflTransGen.single (Or.inl ⟨hs⟩))
    (Relation.ReflTransGen.single (Or.inr ⟨ht⟩))

/-- **Pullback of a free sheaf of modules is free, for any scheme morphism**: for
`φ : T' ⟶ T` and an index type `I`, `φ^*(O_T^{⊕I}) ≅ O_{T'}^{⊕I}`. Built from
`SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso` once `opensMap_final` supplies the `Final` instance.
Project-local. -/
noncomputable def pullbackFreeIso {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) (I : Type u) :
    (Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj (SheafOfModules.free (R := T.ringCatSheaf) I)
      ≅ SheafOfModules.free (R := T'.ringCatSheaf) I := by
  haveI := opensMap_final φ
  exact SheafOfModules.pullbackObjFreeIso φ.toRingCatSheafHom I

/-- **Pullback preserves rank-`d` local freeness.** If `M` is locally free of rank `d` on
`T`, then `φ^* M` is locally free of rank `d` on `T'`, for any scheme morphism `φ`. The
chart cover `{U i}` of `T` trivialising `M` pulls back to the cover `{φ⁻¹ U i}` of `T'`;
on each member the restriction of `φ^* M` is identified with the pulled-back chart-free
sheaf via the pseudofunctor comparison `pullbackComp`, the factorisation
`φ ∘ (φ⁻¹ U i).ι = (φ ∣_ U i) ≫ (U i).ι` (`morphismRestrict_ι`), and `pullbackFreeIso`.
Project-local. -/
lemma pullback_isLocallyFreeOfRank {T' T : Scheme.{u}} (φ : T' ⟶ T) {M : T.Modules}
    {d : ℕ} (h : SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank M d) :
    SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank ((Scheme.Modules.pullback φ).obj M) d := by
  obtain ⟨ι, U, hcover, hloc⟩ := h
  refine ⟨ι, fun i => φ ⁻¹ᵁ (U i), Scheme.Hom.iSup_preimage_eq_top φ hcover, ?_⟩
  intro i
  obtain ⟨e⟩ := hloc i
  exact ⟨(Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (φ ⁻¹ᵁ (U i)).ι φ).app M ≪≫
    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (morphismRestrict_ι φ (U i)).symm).app M ≪≫
    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (φ ∣_ (U i)) (U i).ι).app M).symm ≪≫
    (Scheme.Modules.pullback (φ ∣_ (U i))).mapIso e ≪≫
    pullbackFreeIso (φ ∣_ (U i)) (ULift.{u} (Fin d))⟩

end AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules
